title: Gender and educational attainment
summary: 9699 — gender gaps in achievement, subject choice, and feminist explanations.
learningObjectives: Describe the reversal of the gender gap in attainment.
Explain why girls now outperform boys on average.
Analyse gendered subject choice and career paths.
Evaluate liberal, radical, and postmodern feminist views.

simpleExplanation.title: Girls ahead — but not everywhere
simpleExplanation.summary: Girls now achieve higher grades overall, yet subject stereotypes and workplace inequality persist after school.
steps: Subject choice — STEM vs humanities split.

flashcards.back: Girls now outperform boys at GCSE/A Level in many countries.
Girls' private study vs boys' outdoor leisure — may aid grades.
Sharpe — girls' ambitions shifted from love to careers since 1970s.
Working-class boys reject academic masculinity (Willis, Francis).
Girls dominate humanities/health; boys dominate STEM — stereotypes persist.
Glass ceiling, pay gap — achievement ≠ workplace equality.

sections.content: **Gender and attainment** (6.4) completes Paper 3. Distinguish **achievement** from **subject choice** and **workplace pay gaps**.

sections.question: Assess sociological explanations for girls' improved educational performance relative to boys. [15 marks]
sections.solution:
**Explanations for girls' success:** **Feminism** raised aspirations (Sharpe); **equal opportunities** policies; **coursework** era suited conscientiousness (later reformed); **female role models** in teaching; **GCSE structure**.

**Explanations for boys' relative decline:** **Laddish culture** (Francis); **crisis of masculinity**; **lack of male teachers**; **material** factors for working-class boys overlap with **class**.

**Evaluation:** Gap **not universal** — **class/ethnicity** intersect; girls lead **overall grades** but **STEM subjects** still male-dominated.

**Conclusion:** **Social and cultural shifts** explain trend more than **ability** — patriarchy **transformed in education** but **not eliminated**.
